rows.title: Japan AI Promotion Act: National Diet Adopts Framework Law on Artificial Intelligence
rows.summary:
Japan's National Diet has adopted the Act on Promotion of Research, Development and Utilisation of Artificial Intelligence-Related Technology (commonly referred to as the AI Promotion Act), establishing a national legislative framework for artificial intelligence governance in Japan.

On a date in 2025 confirmed by reference materials published by Kojima Law Offices, the National Diet of Japan enacted the AI Promotion Act, creating the country's first dedicated statutory basis for the promotion, development and regulated use of AI technology across public and private sectors.

The Act sets out a set of foundational principles governing the research, development and utilisation of AI-related technology in Japan. The legislation establishes responsibilities for the national government, local public entities, businesses and users of AI systems, allocating obligations across these categories of actor. The Act provides that the national government bears primary responsibility for formulating and implementing measures to promote AI in a manner consistent with the principles the legislation enumerates, including principles relating to human-centricity, safety, fairness, transparency and accountability. The legislators determined that a coherent national policy framework was necessary to ensure that Japan's AI development proceeds in a manner that protects individuals while supporting economic and social advancement.

The Act further provides for the establishment of governmental measures to support the collection, management and sharing of data necessary for AI development, as well as measures to foster AI literacy and human resources in the field. The legislation addresses international cooperation, directing the national government to work with foreign states and international organisations on AI governance standards. The Kojima Law Offices reference translation notes that the instrument is structured around broad promotional and principled provisions rather than prescriptive operational rules, reflecting a promotional rather than purely regulatory legislative design. No specific penalties or enforcement mechanisms of a criminal or administrative fine nature are enumerated in the framework provisions described in the available reference materials.

rows.title: Japan Digital Agency Concludes Technology-Based Regulatory Reform Committee After Ten Sessions, Reports 98% of National Analogue Regulations Revised
rows.summary:
Japan's Digital Agency has concluded the fourth and final session of its Technology-Based Regulatory Reform Promotion Committee, convened under the Digital System Reform Study Group, with the Digital Agency reporting that 98% of identified national analogue regulations requiring revision have been completed, and the programme's Technology Catalogue reaching 218 entries.

On 19 March 2026, the Digital Agency convened the fourth session of the Technology-Based Regulatory Reform Promotion Committee online, with Professor Hiroshi Esaki of the University of Tokyo Graduate School of Information Science and Technology serving as chair. The secretariat confirmed at the session that this meeting constituted the final instalment of the committee, which had held a total of ten sessions including its predecessor body under the Digital Rincho framework.

The Digital Agency's secretariat presented materials summarising the programme's origins, scope and outcomes. The agency notes that the initiative was established to address analogue regulations obstructing the implementation of digital technologies, with the objective of creating a regulatory environment adapted to technological progress. The agency states that, at the national level, a consolidated revision plan was established to reform relevant legislation in bulk, and a Digital Legal Examination process was introduced to prevent new analogue regulations from arising. The agency further notes that support for local governments in revising analogue regulations in prefectural and municipal ordinances has been substantially strengthened over the programme's duration.

According to materials presented by the secretariat, the Digital Agency identified approximately 10,000 analogue regulatory provisions at the outset, of which around 1,000 were assessed as requiring technical verification. The agency states that roughly half of those provisions were subject to technical verification conducted directly by the Digital Agency, supported by dedicated budget allocations across multiple fiscal years. The agency reports that the Technology Catalogue, covering seven categories of digital technology including construction and sewerage sectors, reached 218 entries by the end of the current fiscal year, though the agency notes that demand for new registrations had stabilised and new submissions were accordingly closed in February 2026. The agency also states that approximately 80% of technology verification cases led to practical implementation examples, a result the committee noted as demonstrating tangible efficacy for end users.

rows.title: Japan AI Safety Institute Publishes Second Edition of Known Attacks and Their Impacts on AI Systems
rows.summary:
Japan's AI Safety Institute (AISI Japan) has published the second edition of its technical reference document, "Known Attacks and Their Impacts on AI Systems," cataloguing twelve categories of attack specific to AI systems and mapping their effects across confidentiality, integrity, and availability dimensions.

On 22 April 2026, AISI Japan released the updated document, building on the first edition published in March 2025. The document carries no fine or enforcement mechanism and imposes no legal obligations; it is a technical reference intended to support the examination of countermeasures by developers, operators, and others involved in AI system deployment.

AISI Japan identifies twelve attack types, designated A through L, organised across four attack surfaces: Training, Model, Input, and Output. AISI Japan finds that attacks within scope are those involving intervention in an AI model's training or inference processes, or the exploitation and tampering of its inputs and outputs. AISI Japan notes that attacks that target AI model assets through conventional network vulnerabilities, without interfering with training or inference, fall outside the document's scope. AISI Japan notes that the assumed AI system encompasses both a development and training environment and an operational environment, and that the framework applies to AI models beyond large language models, including systems that incorporate plugins, internal knowledge bases, and external knowledge sources.

AISI Japan determines that the twelve attack types include Data Poisoning (D) and Model Poisoning (C) during training; Model Extraction (A), Training and Reference Information Gathering (B), Prompt Injection (H), Prompt Stealing (G), Evasion (E), and Energy-Latency attacks (F) during inference; and Side-Channel attacks (K), Post-training Model Manipulation (J), Code Injection (I), and Knowledge Poisoning (L) affecting model and knowledge components. AISI Japan states that the document maps each attack type to specific impacts, including model leakage, training data leakage, safeguard bypass, model malfunction, system compromise, computational waste, task abandonment, failure of unlearning, and unintended refusal. AISI Japan states that system compromise, classified under integrity, can extend to confidentiality and availability impacts and can enable conventional cyberattacks. AISI Japan illustrates, by way of example, that training data leakage from AI used in medical diagnosis raises privacy concerns, and that abnormal outputs in automated driving systems present safety risks.

rows.title: Japan AI Safety Institute Publishes Japanese-Language Guide to AI System Attack Types and Security Impacts
rows.summary:
The Japan AI Safety Institute (J-AISI), operating under the Information-technology Promotion Agency (IPA), has published a Japanese-language guidance document cataloguing eleven categories of known attacks against AI systems, mapping each to confidentiality, integrity and availability impacts.

On 6 February 2026, J-AISI published "Securing AI Systems: A Guide to Known Attacks and Impacts (Japanese Version)", authored by Naoto Kiribuchi, Kengo Zenitani and Takayuki Semitsu of J-AISI and IPA. The document is presented as a Japanese translation and detailed version of a previously published J-AISI reference, itself corresponding to an arXiv technical paper (arXiv:2506.23296v1). No financial penalties are associated with this publication.

J-AISI identifies eleven principal attack types applicable to both predictive and generative AI systems, designated attacks A through K in the document. These include model extraction attacks, training data inference, model poisoning, data poisoning, evasion attacks, sponge attacks, prompt theft, prompt injection, code injection, fine-tuning attacks and rowhammer attacks. J-AISI maps each attack type to one or more of three security impact categories: information disclosure, system compromise and computational resource exhaustion, which the document aligns explicitly with the confidentiality, integrity and availability (CIA) triad.

J-AISI states that the guide is directed at researchers, developers, security practitioners and policymakers, including those without specialist AI security expertise, with the stated purpose of supporting recognition of AI-specific risks and implementation of effective countermeasures. The document notes that J-AISI finds existing academic surveys have addressed either predictive or generative AI systems in isolation, and that a practically oriented guide covering both system types, with explicit linkage between attack mechanisms and security impacts, addresses a gap in available resources. J-AISI further identifies, for each attack type, the attacker capabilities required as preconditions for a successful attack, and organises referenced attack literature by data modality and AI model type to assist practitioners conducting risk assessments and red-team exercises. The document references established international frameworks including NIST AI 100-2e2025 and MITRE ATLAS as part of its contextual analysis.

rows.title: Japan's Personal Information Protection Commission Sets Out Triennial Review Reform Policy for APPI
rows.summary:
Japan's Personal Information Protection Commission has published the outline of its policy for amending the Act on the Protection of Personal Information, derived from the triennial review obligation under Article 10 of the supplementary provisions of the 2020 amended APPI, covering consent exemptions, children's data protections, biometric data restrictions, enforcement powers and a new surcharge mechanism.

On 9 January 2026, the Personal Information Protection Commission published the outline of its system reform policy based on the triennial review provision (Supplementary Provisions, Art. 10) inserted by the 2020 amendment to the Act on the Protection of Personal Information. The Commission states that the reform reflects rapid developments in information and communications technology as well as international trends.

The Commission determines that several consent requirements are to be revised. The Commission states that the policy provides that third-party provision of personal data and acquisition of publicly available sensitive personal information will not require the data subject's consent where the data is used solely for the creation of statistical information, including AI development classified as statistical processing. The Commission also finds that consent requirements for purpose-of-use violations, acquisition of sensitive personal information and third-party transfers are to be relaxed where handling clearly does not harm the rights and interests of the individual concerned. The Commission states that where the data subject is under 16 years of age, consent and notification obligations are to be directed explicitly to the legal representative, and a duty provision requiring priority consideration of the best interests of the minor is to be introduced.

On enforcement, the Commission determines that facial feature data and equivalent information will be subject to mandatory disclosure obligations, and that opt-out third-party transfers of such data will be prohibited. The Commission further states that a surcharge mechanism is to be introduced requiring payment of an amount equivalent to financial gains derived from serious violations that infringe individual rights and interests through large-scale, malicious handling of personal information. The Commission also states that the policy provides for revised order requirements to enable faster correction of violations and new penalties for malicious-purpose provision of personal information databases and fraudulent acquisition of personal information.

rows.title: Japan Enacts Cyber Defence Legislation for Critical Infrastructure
rows.summary:
Japan's Cabinet Secretariat National Cyber Policy Office has enacted the Act on Prevention of Damage Caused by Unauthorised Acts Against Important Computers (Act No. 42 of 2025) and its accompanying arrangement act (Act No. 43 of 2025), establishing a legal framework for active cyber defence of critical electronic systems.

On 23 May 2025, the Cabinet Secretariat National Cyber Policy Office promulgated the legislation following parliamentary deliberation and amendment. The bills were approved by cabinet on 7 February 2025. The authority notes that the legislation implements commitments made under the National Security Strategy adopted by cabinet decision on 16 December 2022, which set a target of raising Japan's cyber security response capacity to a level at or above that of major Western nations.

The authority finds that the legislation addresses three core areas: enhanced public-private cooperation, the use of communications information, and access and neutralisation measures. Under the public-private cooperation provisions, operators of critical infrastructure are required to notify the competent minister upon introducing specified important computers and to report incidents affecting those systems to both the competent minister and the Prime Minister. The authority finds that operators of qualifying critical infrastructure must file asset notifications setting out product names and related details, and must report cybersecurity incidents or events capable of causing such incidents. The legislation provides that the Prime Minister and the competent minister for computer supply businesses may provide vulnerability information to suppliers of computers or programmes used in critical systems.

The authority notes that the legislation includes a provision requiring that powers under the act be exercised only to the minimum extent necessary for the stated purposes and that the confidentiality of communications and other constitutional rights must not be unduly restricted. The arrangement act provides for neutralisation measures by police against confirmed attack servers, subject to prior approval by an independent body and direction by the Commissioner-General of the National Police Agency.

rows.title: Japan AI Safety Institute Publishes Overview of Known Attacks and Impacts on AI Systems
rows.summary:
The Japan AI Safety Institute (AISI Japan) has published a technical document cataloguing known attack types targeting AI systems and their associated impacts, drawing on academic papers and other sources to inform the examination of countermeasures.

On 19 March 2025, AISI Japan released "Known Attacks and Their Impacts on AI Systems (V1)" to provide practitioners and developers with a structured overview of AI-specific security threats. The document does not carry a fine or enforcement determination; it is a technical guidance publication.

AISI Japan identifies eleven categories of impact arising from attacks on AI systems, spanning confidentiality, integrity and availability. Under confidentiality, the Institute identifies model leakage, training data leakage, application information leakage, input information leakage and internal data leakage as potential consequences of attacks. Under integrity, AISI Japan identifies that model malfunction, interpretability malfunction and safeguard bypass represent distinct adverse outcomes. Availability-related impacts identified by the Institute include computational waste, internal data corruption and system compromise, with the document noting that system compromise can itself lead to any category of impact.

The document maps eleven named attack vectors to these impact categories. AISI Japan describes attacks occurring during the training phase, including data poisoning (Attack D), model poisoning (Attack C), adversarial fine-tuning (Attack J) and code injection (Attack I). During inference, the Institute identifies model extraction (Attack A), training data-related information gathering (Attack B), evasion attacks (Attack E), energy-latency attacks (Attack F), prompt stealing (Attack G), prompt injection (Attack H) and Rowhammer attacks (Attack K). The document explicitly limits its scope to attacks that involve intervening in an AI model's training or inference processes, or that exploit and tamper with its inputs and outputs, and excludes conventional cybersecurity attacks such as theft of AI models through unauthorised network access, which AISI Japan notes do not involve interference with training or inference processes.

AISI Japan states that the real-world consequences of these attacks include privacy violations arising from training data leaks in medical AI systems and safety risks from abnormal outputs in automated driving systems, and characterises AI-specific security measures as essential given the increasing frequency with which such attacks are reported as AI systems evolve.
